food_subgroup: Reptile / turtle-based braised and stewed dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for the composite dish 'Stewed Soft-Shelled Turtle with Ham'. The closest defensible anchor is the turtle meat food product class. The composite dish includes Jinhua ham, aromatics, and broth.
ontology.foodex2_description: Turtle meat — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the primary protein component of this dish. No composite dish code exists in FoodEx2 for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for 'Stewed Soft-Shelled Turtle with Ham'. Code A0FJP (turtle meat) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. The ham and broth components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for soft-shelled turtle meat or this composite dish. SNOMED CT does not cover this food category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Stewed Soft-Shelled Turtle with Ham is approximately 400 g, comprising turtle meat, ham slices, bamboo shoots, and clear collagen-rich broth served in a deep bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Turtle, meat only, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 17179); supplemented with Ham, cured, extra lean, canned — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 07025) for the ham component
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or approved-source entry exists for the composite dish 'Stewed Soft-Shelled Turtle with Ham'. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy turtle meat (raw, NDB 17179) as the primary protein base, adjusted for cooking losses from slow-stewing (approximately 20–25% moisture loss in meat, offset by broth absorption), and blended with a proportional contribution from cured ham (NDB 07025) and minor aromatics. Sodium is elevated relative to raw turtle due to the salt-cured Jinhua ham and concentrated broth. Saturated fat is estimated from the combined fat profile of turtle meat and ham. All values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the finished dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat-based stew category
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC — Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Stewed Soft-Shelled Turtle with Ham in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate meat-based stews and soups (GI range 10–20 in published tables). The dish contains only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from bamboo shoots and trace aromatics, with negligible glycaemic impact. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (15 × 4.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.72, rounded to 0.7. This is classified as very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450 mg per 100 g; ~1800 mg per 400 g serving) from salt-cured Jinhua ham and concentrated collagen broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 100–150 g and the broth should be avoided, with total daily sodium intake monitored carefully against the recommended ceiling of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) and high-quality protein (14.5 g per 100 g) with a low estimated glycemic index of 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its very low carbohydrate content and high protein density produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion; however, the high sodium content requires monitoring in patients with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium from cured ham poses a cardiovascular risk; fat content is moderate with an estimated saturated fat contribution from both turtle meat and ham
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish infrequently and in small portions due to its high sodium content (~1800 mg per serving), which can elevate blood pressure; the collagen and lean protein from turtle meat may be beneficial, but the cured ham component should be minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in collagen and anti-inflammatory ginger, but high sodium and cured meat may be pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The collagen-rich turtle meat and ginger aromatics may support joint health, but the high sodium content and processed cured ham can exacerbate inflammation in some individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; consume in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stewed Soft-Shelled Turtle with Ham (火腿炖甲鱼, Huǒtuǐ dùn jiǎyú) is a prestigious slow-simmered dish from Anhui (Huizhou) cuisine in East-Central China, historically served as a premium tonic course at banquets and during convalescence. The dish is built around fresh soft-shelled turtle (Pelodiscus sinensis), prized in Chinese culinary and medicinal tradition for its gelatinous skirt, tender meat, and high collagen content. The turtle is cleaned and blanched, then combined with slices of aged, salt-cured Jinhua ham — one of China's most celebrated cured meats — along with ginger, scallions, and bamboo shoots in a clay pot or covered casserole. The preparation is slow-stewed over low heat for two to three hours until the broth becomes rich, golden, and deeply flavoured with dissolved collagen and the savoury umami of the ham. The resulting soup is clear yet intensely flavoured, with tender, slightly gelatinous turtle meat and silky broth. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (5.5 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (1.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. It is a notable source of selenium (18.5 mcg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg per 100 g), niacin (4.5 mg per 100 g), and iron (1.8 mg per 100 g). The primary clinical caution is its very high sodium content (~450 mg per 100 g), attributable to the salt-cured ham and concentrated broth, which makes it unsuitable for individuals on strict low-sodium diets or those managing hypertension or heart failure. The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is approximately 0.7, making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when sodium is not a concern.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy turtle meat (NDB 17179) and cured ham (NDB 07025) with proportional blending and cooking-loss adjustments. The GI is an analogous estimate from published high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat stew data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary ingredient (turtle meat) has a direct USDA SR Legacy entry and the adaptation methodology is documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific stewed composite dish (soft-shelled turtle with Jinhua ham) are not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. The USDA SR Legacy entry for turtle meat (NDB 17179) does not include a full fatty acid breakdown or cholesterol value for the cooked composite preparation; water content of the finished stewed dish is likewise not reported in any approved source consulted. Saturated fat (1.6 g per 100 g) is estimated from the combined fat profiles of turtle meat and cured ham from USDA SR Legacy and is the only fatty acid fraction supportable from available data. The input dietary_tags incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan' for this dish, which contains turtle meat and cured pork ham; these tags have been removed. The duplicate portion entry in the input has been deduplicated to a single 400 g serving. The source cited in the input (China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences, http://www.cacms.ac.cn/) is not on the approved source list for Project Veritas v2 and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central as the primary nutrient evidence source. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301116 (turtle meat food product) and FoodEx2 code A0FJP (turtle meat) are used as approximate anchors for the primary ingredient; no composite dish code exists in either system for this preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized record to RESULT template structure; replaced non-approved input source with USDA FoodData Central; corrected incorrect vegetarian/vegan dietary tags; deduplicated portion entries; added ontology anchors with gap notes.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; ensured -1 values in per_100g were referenced in notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document the missing-data basis for all five -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, water_g), naming the specific approved source consulted (USDA SR Legacy NDB 17179) and confirming these fields are not reported therein, thereby satisfying G3 numeric hygiene requirements.
